perturbation.original_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained) is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation or alleged violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that, in addition to other costs and damages, all reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees incurred by the party prevailing in such action shall be paid by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained) is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation or alleged violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that costs and damages shall be determined at the court's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the guaranteed payment of 'all reasonable costs and attorneys' fees' to the prevailing party, the contract contradicts the Michigan Wage and Fringe Benefits Act which ensures workers are fully compensated when a company withholds benefits. This omission could financially harm Kennedy if the company sues him and he wins, because it is no longer a certainty that the company will cover legal fees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Michigan Wage and Fringe Benefits Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Michigan Compiled Laws § 408.481 ensures the payment of fringe benefits as part of wages. Removing the guarantee of covering the prevailing party's legal fees could effectively reduce the total compensation received in case of a dispute, contradicting the spirit of this law which mandates full compensation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 408.481 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 408.481 Download Section Chapter 408 Act 390 of 1978 Previous Section Next Section PAYMENT OF WAGES AND FRINGE BENEFITS (EXCERPT) Act 390 of 1978 408.481 Complaint; filing; form; notice to employer; investigation; informal resolution of dispute; notice to employer and employee; request for review; oaths and affirmations; subpoena; witnesses; evidence; parties to proceeding; appointment and duties of hearings officer; hearing; determination; judicial review; venue. Sec. 11. (1) An employee who believes that his or her employer has violated this act may file a written complaint with the department within 12 months after the alleged violation. A complaint filed under section 13(2) shall be filed within 30 days after the alleged violation occurs. Bilingual complaint forms shall be provided by the department in those areas where substantial numbers of non-English speaking employees are employed. (2) Within a reasonable time after a complaint is filed the department shall notify the employer and investigate the claim and shall attempt to informally resolve the dispute. (3) If the department is unable to informally resolve the dispute, the department shall notify the employer and employee within 90 days after the complaint is filed. The notification shall include a determination of the merits of the complaint and shall cite the specific violation, if any, wages and fringe benefits due, and specific penalties assessed. (4) The employer or employee may request a review of the department's determination within 14 days after notification is issued. If a request for a review by either the employer or employee is not received by the department within 14 days, in the absence of a showing of good cause for a late request, the department's determination is final. (5) For the purpose of an investigation or proceeding under this act, the director of labor or an authorized representative of the director may administer oaths and affirmations, subpo
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'all reasonable costs and attorneys' fees' creates a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this omission could financially harm Kennedy if the company sues and he wins, because it removes the certainty of the company covering legal fees. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the Michigan Wage and Fringe Benefits Act, which ensures workers are fully compensated when a company withholds benefits. Snippet 1, MCL 408.481, addresses complaints related to violations of the Act, implying a remedy for such violations, which is undermined by removing guaranteed coverage of legal fees. This makes the contract non-compliant and contradicts a legally mandated protection, according to the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers, and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given (i) when delivered by hand (with written confirmation of receipt); (ii) when received by the addressee if sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier (receipt requested); (iii) on the date sent by facsimile or e-mail of a PDF document (with confirmation of transmission) if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, and on the next Business Day if sent after normal business hours of the recipient or (iv) on the third (3rd) day after the date mailed, by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers, and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given when mailed by standard mail.
perturbation.explanation: By only allowing standard mail for communications, the contract contradicts the principle of 'reasonable notification' required in many legal contexts under Delaware law. Many business communications require proof of delivery, which standard mail does not provide. This can be detrimental to Kennedy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law relating to notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires reasonable notification, depending on context. This change to the contract removes guaranteed confirmation of important business communication, potentially violating this requirement. Kennedy could miss deadlines or other actions because of non-receipt of communication, creating a legal risk.

General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such share
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided several methods for delivering notices, including methods ensuring receipt confirmation. The changed text limits acceptable delivery to standard mail, which does not guarantee confirmation. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate Delaware law's requirement for reasonable notification by potentially causing missed deadlines due to non-receipt of communication. Snippet 1 discusses mergers and consolidations, and while seemingly unrelated, the core principle is that Delaware law requires a certain level of formality and documented agreement in corporate actions. Reducing communication to standard mail undermines the verifiable nature of important notifications, presenting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and interpreted in accordance with the substantive laws of the state of Delaware, without regard to the choice or conflict of law rules of such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and interpreted according to the Company's interpretation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to Delaware law creates ambiguity and allows the Company to potentially interpret the agreement in a way that is unfavorable to Kennedy, which contradicts the principle of fair dealing and good faith implied in contracts, which can be found in Delaware case law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Delaware Law
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing only the Company to interpret the contract, the altered clause introduces a conflict of interest. The Delaware Supreme Court has established the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing to ensure parties do not use contractual gaps to deprive the other party of the benefit of their bargain. This modified clause violates this covenant because the company could interpret the contract to Kennedy's disadvantage.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates adherence to Delaware law, including its conflict of law rules. The changed text removes this requirement, substituting it with the 'Company's interpretation.' This omission creates a legal loophole, potentially allowing the Company to interpret the agreement unfairly, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained by the LLM. While no specific law snippet is provided, the Perturbation Explanation highlights a violation of fair dealing, which is recognized in Delaware case law. This directly contradicts the principle of adhering to established legal standards, making the modification a serious legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Kennedy acknowledges that a breach or threatened breach of this Agreement would give rise to irreparable harm to the Company, for which monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy, and hereby agrees that in the event of a breach or a threatened breach by Kennedy of any such obligations, the Company shall, in addition to any and all other rights and remedies that may be available to it in respect of such breach, be entitled to equitable relief, including a temporary restraining order, an injunction, specific performance, and any other relief that may be available from a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Kennedy acknowledges that a breach of this Agreement would allow the Company to pursue remedies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the acknowledgment that monetary damages may not be adequate and eliminating the explicit entitlement to equitable relief (like injunctions), the clause weakens the Company's ability to enforce the non-compete agreement. In Michigan, non-compete agreements must be reasonable and protect a legitimate business interest. Removing the specific remedies and stating Kennedy's breach would give rise to 'irreparable harm' makes the agreement more difficult for the company to enforce, potentially conflicting with state law on enforcing reasonable non-competes.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL 445.774a allows for reasonable covenants not to compete. By removing the acknowledgement of irreparable harm and entitlement to injunctive relief, the contract weakens its enforceability under Michigan law. A Michigan court may be less likely to grant injunctive relief if the contract doesn't explicitly state it as a possible remedy and if the clause acknowledges the impossibility of irreparable harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.774a - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.774a Download Section Chapter 445 Act 274 of 1984 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN ANTITRUST REFORM ACT (EXCERPT) Act 274 of 1984 445.774a Agreement or covenant protecting business interests of employer; applicability of section. Sec. 4a. (1) An employer may obtain from an employee an agreement or covenant which protects an employer's reasonable competitive business interests and expressly prohibits an employee from engaging in employment or a line of business after termination of employment if the agreement or covenant is reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business. To the extent any such agreement or covenant is found to be unreasonable in any respect, a court may limit the agreement to render it reasonable in light of the circumstances in which it was made and specifically enforce the agreement as limited. (2) This section shall apply to covenants and agreements which are entered into after March 29, 1985. History: Add. 1987, Act 243, Imd. Eff. Dec. 28, 1987
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit acknowledgment of 'irreparable harm' and the entitlement to equitable relief, which weakens the enforceability of the non-compete agreement under Michigan law. MCL 445.774a, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows employers to obtain agreements protecting reasonable competitive business interests. By omitting the acknowledgment of irreparable harm, the modified contract makes it more difficult for the company to demonstrate that a breach would cause such harm, potentially leading a court to find the agreement unreasonable or unenforceable. The original text ensures the company can pursue equitable relief, including injunctions, whereas the changed text only allows the company to 'pursue remedies,' making the agreement's enforceability less clear and predictable, contradicting the intent of MCL 445.774a to protect reasonable business interests.
